content.problemStatement:
A non-negative number is called "SSS semi-palindromic" if it is divisible by SSS and it is possible to rearrange its digits in such a way that the resulting number is palindromic. If the initial number had DDD digits (without leading zeroes), consider the number obtained by rearranging to have exactly DDD digits as well (leading zeroes are allowed).

Compute the number of "SSS semi-palindromic" numbers smaller than 10K10^K10K.
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The first and only line of each test case contains two space-separated integers S and K.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer — the answer modulo 10^9 + 7.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The semi-palindromic numbers for each test case are counted and printed.
The semi-palindromic numbers for each test case are counted and printed.
The semi-palindromic numbers for the test case are counted and printed.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we need to find all semi-palindromic numbers smaller than 10^K. A semi-palindromic number is a number that is divisible by S and can be rearranged to form a palindrome. We can use a brute force approach to generate all possible numbers and check if they are semi-palindromic. However, this approach would be inefficient for large values of K. A more efficient approach would be to use a recursive function to generate all possible palindromes and check if they are divisible by S.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * 10^K) — the time complexity is dominated by the generation of all possible palindromes.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(10^K) — the space complexity is dominated by the storage of all possible palindromes.
